Baseline measurements show **extremely fast parsing** - average 2-4 microseconds per operation.\n10\t\n11\t## Methodology\n12\t\n13\t- **Sampling:** 50 iterations per test case for statistical significance\n14\t- **Measurement:** `time.perf_counter()` for high-precision timing\n15\t- **Test Cases:**\n16\t  1. Simple fenced JSON (single intent)\n17\t  2. Simple bare JSON (single intent, no fences)\n18\t  3. Complex fenced JSON (multiple intents)\n19\t\n20\t## Baseline Results\n21\t\n22\t### Simple Fenced JSON (Single Intent)\n23\t\n24\t| Metric | Value |\n25\t|--------|-------|\n26\t| **Average Total Time** | **0.0028 ms (2.8 μs)** |\n27\t| Median | 0.0023 ms |\n28\t| 95th percentile | 0.0071 ms |\n29\t| Max | 0.0143 ms |\n30\t| Std Dev | 0.0019 ms |\n31\t\n32\t**Breakdown:**\n33\t- Fence removal: 0.0006 ms (20.8%)\n34\t- JSON parsing: 0.0022 ms (79.2%)\n35\t\n36\t### Simple Bare JSON (Single Intent, No Fences)\n37\t\n38\t| Metric | Value |\n39\t|--------|-------|\n40\t| **Average Total Time** | **0.0021 ms (2.1 μs)** |\n41\t| Median | 0.0020 ms |\n42\t| 95th percentile | 0.0027 ms |\n43\t| Max | 0.0060 ms |\n44\t| Std Dev | 0.0006 ms |\n45\t\n46\t**Breakdown:**\n47\t- Fence removal: 0.0002 ms (9.5%)\n48\t- JSON parsing: 0.0019 ms (90.5%)\n49\t\n50\t### Complex Fenced JSON (Multiple Intents)\n51\t\n52\t| Metric | Value |\n53\t|--------|-------|\n54\t| **Average Total Time** | **0.0036 ms (3.6 μs)** |\n55\t| Median | 0.0034 ms |\n56\t| 95th percentile | 0.0063 ms |\n57\t| Max | 0.0086 ms |\n58\t| Std Dev | 0.0010 ms |\n59\t\n60\t**Breakdown:**\n61\t- Fence removal: 0.0005 ms (15.0%)\n62\t- JSON parsing: 0.0031 ms (85.0%)\n63\t\n64\t## Comparison Table\n65\t\n66\t| Test Case | Total (ms) | Fence (ms) | JSON (ms) | Fence % |\n67\t|-----------|------------|------------|-----------|---------|\n68\t| Simple bare JSON | 0.0020 | 0.0002 | 0.0018 | 9.5% |\n69\t| Simple fenced JSON | 0.0024 | 0.0005 | 0.0019 | 20.8% |\n70\t| Complex fenced JSON | 0.0035 | 0.0005 | 0.0030 | 15.0% |\n71\t\n72\t## Identified Bottlenecks\n73\t\n74\t### 1. JSON Parsing (85-90% of time)\n75\t- **Impact:** Primary bottleneck - 79-90% of total parse time\n76\t- **Cause:** Standard `json.loads()` is already highly optimized\n77\t- **Opportunity:** Minimal - CPython's json module is already C-accelerated\n78\t\n79\t### 2. Fence Removal (9-21% of time)\n80\t- **Impact:** Secondary bottleneck - varies by response type\n81\t- **Cause:** String operations (strip, find, rfind, slice)\n82\t- **Opportunity:** Already optimized with `find()`/`rfind()` vs. `split()`/`rsplit()`\n83\t- **Current approach:** Position-based single-pass (10-15% faster than split-based)\n84\t\n85\t### 3. Current Implementation Strengths\n86\t- **Optimized fence removal:** Uses `find()`/`rfind()` instead of `split()`/`rsplit()`\n87\t- **No regex overhead:** Avoids expensive regex patterns\n88\t- **Single-pass parsing:** Direct slice extraction without intermediate allocations\n89\t- **Early returns:** Empty/whitespace checks avoid unnecessary processing\n90\t\n91\t## Before/After Measurement Strategy\n92\t\n93\tFor any optimization work, use the following strategy:\n94\t\n95\t1. **Capture baseline:** Run `profile_json_parsing.py` before changes\n96\t2. **Implement optimization:** Modify parsing logic\n97\t3. **Rerun profiling:** Execute same test cases with new implementation\n98\t4. **Compare results:**\n99\t   - Average time improvement (%)\n100\t   - 95th percentile improvement\n101\t   - Code complexity change\n102\t\n103\t## Key Findings\n104\t\n105\t1. **Parsing is already very fast:** 2-4 μs average is negligible compared to LLM latency (hundreds of ms)\n106\t2. **JSON parsing dominates:** 85-90% of time is in `json.loads()`, not fence removal\n107\t3. **Fence removal is minor:** Even for fenced responses, fence removal is only 15-21% of total time\n108\t4. **Optimization headroom is limited:** Current implementation is already near-optimal for Python\n109\t5. **LLM latency dominates:** Total parse time (2-4 μs) is << 1% of typical LLM response time (200-500 ms)\n110\t\n111\t## Recommendations\n112\t\n113\t1. **Accept current performance:** 2-4 μs parsing time is not a bottleneck\n114\t2. **Focus elsewhere:** Optimize LLM call latency, not JSON parsing\n115\t3. **Profile only if needed:** Re-run profiling only if parsing becomes a measurable issue\n116\t4. **Avoid premature optimization:** Current implementation is already optimized\n117\t\n118\t## Testing\n119\t\n120\tTo re-run this baseline:\n121\t\n122\t```bash\n123\tcd /home/coding/aide-de-camp\n124\t.venv/bin/python profile_json_parsing.py\n125\t```\n126\t\n127\t## Related Work\n128\t\n129\t- Response parser implementation: `src/llm/response_parser.py`\n130\t- Intent router usage: `src/intent/router.py:236`\n131\t- Previous optimization work: `notes/adc-3e5gg.md` (fence removal JSON Parsing Performance Baseline\n\n**Date:** 2026-07-24\n**Component:** Intent Router JSON Parsing\n**Location:** `src/intent/router.py` line 236, `src/llm/response_parser.py`\n\n## Summary\n\nCurrent JSON parsing implementation uses an optimized `find()`/`rfind()` approach for fence removal and standard `json.loads()` for parsing. Baseline measurements show **extremely fast parsing** - average 2-4 microseconds per operation.\n\n## Methodology\n\n- **Sampling:** 50 iterations per test case for statistical significance\n- **Measurement:** `time.perf_counter()` for high-precision timing\n- **Test Cases:**\n  1. Simple fenced JSON (single intent)\n  2. Simple bare JSON (single intent, no fences)\n  3. Complex fenced JSON (multiple intents)\n\n## Baseline Results\n\n### Simple Fenced JSON (Single Intent)\n\n| Metric | Value |\n|--------|-------|\n| **Average Total Time** | **0.0028 ms (2.8 μs)** |\n| Median | 0.0023 ms |\n| 95th percentile | 0.0071 ms |\n| Max | 0.0143 ms |\n| Std Dev | 0.0019 ms |\n\n**Breakdown:**\n- Fence removal: 0.0006 ms (20.8%)\n- JSON parsing: 0.0022 ms (79.2%)\n\n### Simple Bare JSON (Single Intent, No Fences)\n\n| Metric | Value |\n|--------|-------|\n| **Average Total Time** | **0.0021 ms (2.1 μs)** |\n| Median | 0.0020 ms |\n| 95th percentile | 0.0027 ms |\n| Max | 0.0060 ms |\n| Std Dev | 0.0006 ms |\n\n**Breakdown:**\n- Fence removal: 0.0002 ms (9.5%)\n- JSON parsing: 0.0019 ms (90.5%)\n\n### Complex Fenced JSON (Multiple Intents)\n\n| Metric | Value |\n|--------|-------|\n| **Average Total Time** | **0.0036 ms (3.6 μs)** |\n| Median | 0.0034 ms |\n| 95th percentile | 0.0063 ms |\n| Max | 0.0086 ms |\n| Std Dev | 0.0010 ms |\n\n**Breakdown:**\n- Fence removal: 0.0005 ms (15.0%)\n- JSON parsing: 0.0031 ms (85.0%)\n\n## Comparison Table\n\n| Test Case | Total (ms) | Fence (ms) | JSON (ms) | Fence % |\n|-----------|------------|------------|-----------|---------|\n| Simple bare JSON | 0.0020 | 0.0002 | 0.0018 | 9.5% |\n| Simple fenced JSON | 0.0024 | 0.0005 | 0.0019 | 20.8% |\n| Complex fenced JSON | 0.0035 | 0.0005 | 0.0030 | 15.0% |\n\n## Identified Bottlenecks\n\n### 1. JSON Parsing (85-90% of time)\n- **Impact:** Primary bottleneck - 79-90% of total parse time\n- **Cause:** Standard `json.loads()` is already highly optimized\n- **Opportunity:** Minimal - CPython's json module is already C-accelerated\n\n### 2. Fence Removal (9-21% of time)\n- **Impact:** Secondary bottleneck - varies by response type\n- **Cause:** String operations (strip, find, rfind, slice)\n- **Opportunity:** Already optimized with `find()`/`rfind()` vs. `split()`/`rsplit()`\n- **Current approach:** Position-based single-pass (10-15% faster than split-based)\n\n### 3. Current Implementation Strengths\n- **Optimized fence removal:** Uses `find()`/`rfind()` instead of `split()`/`rsplit()`\n- **No regex overhead:** Avoids expensive regex patterns\n- **Single-pass parsing:** Direct slice extraction without intermediate allocations\n- **Early returns:** Empty/whitespace checks avoid unnecessary processing\n\n## Before/After Measurement Strategy\n\nFor any optimization work, use the following strategy:\n\n1. **Capture baseline:** Run `profile_json_parsing.py` before changes\n2. **Implement optimization:** Modify parsing logic\n3. **Rerun profiling:** Execute same test cases with new implementation\n4. **Compare results:**\n   - Average time improvement (%)\n   - 95th percentile improvement\n   - Code complexity change\n\n## Key Findings\n\n1. **Parsing is already very fast:** 2-4 μs average is negligible compared to LLM latency (hundreds of ms)\n2. **JSON parsing dominates:** 85-90% of time is in `json.loads()`, not fence removal\n3. **Fence removal is minor:** Even for fenced responses, fence removal is only 15-21% of total time\n4. **Optimization headroom is limited:** Current implementation is already near-optimal for Python\n5. **LLM latency dominates:** Total parse time (2-4 μs) is << 1% of typical LLM response time (200-500 ms)\n\n## Recommendations\n\n1. **Accept current performance:** 2-4 μs parsing time is not a bottleneck\n2. **Focus elsewhere:** Optimize LLM call latency, not JSON parsing\n3. **Profile only if needed:** Re-run profiling only if parsing becomes a measurable issue\n4. **Avoid premature optimization:** Current implementation is already optimized\n\n## Testing\n\nTo re-run this baseline:\n\n```bash\ncd /home/coding/aide-de-camp\n.venv/bin/python profile_json_parsing.py\n```\n\n## Related Work\n\n- Response parser implementation: `src/llm/response_parser.py`\n- Intent router usage: `src/intent/router.py:236`\n- Previous optimization work: `notes/adc-3e5gg.md` (fence removal optimization)\n","numLines":132,"startLine":1,"totalLines":132}}}

{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_0f656f94715d4187a16a85ed","type":"tool_result","content":"1\t\"\"\"\n2\tZAI Response Parser - Centralized utility for parsing LLM responses.\n3\t\n4\tHandles:\n5\t1. ZAI proxy response unwrapping (removes outer \"result\" field)\n6\t2. Markdown code fence stripping (```json, ```, etc.)\n7\t3. JSON parsing with clear error messages\n8\t4. Graceful fallback for malformed responses\n9\t\n10\tThis utility is used across:\n11\t- Intent Router (src/intent/router.py)\n12\t- Synthesize Strand (src/synthesize/strand.py)\n13\t- Self-Modification Agent (src/agents/self_modification.py)\n14\t- Any other LLM interaction points\n15\t\n16\t## Error Handling Patterns\n17\t\n18\tWhen ParseLLMError is raised, choose recovery strategy based on context:\n19\t\n20\t### Corrective Retry Pattern (Router-style)\n21\tUse when:\n22\t- LLM call is early in pipeline (cheap to retry)\n23\t- Failure cascades to downstream operations\n24\t- Retrying doesn't require re-running expensive operations\n25\t\n26\tExample: Intent router retries once because malformed response affects all downstream processing.\n27\t\n28\tSee: src/intent/router.py classify_utterance()\n29\t\n30\t### Fallback Result Pattern (Synthesize-style)\n31\tUse when:\n32\t- LLM call is AFTER expensive operations (fetch, DB queries, etc.)\n33\t- Data already obtained should not be discarded\n34\t- User should see partial results in degraded-state UX\n35\t\n36\tExample: Synthesize strand returns fallback result because fetch operations already completed.\n37\t\n38\tSee: src/synthesize/strand.py synthesize()\n39\t\n40\t### Documentation\n41\tSee: docs/error-handling-standardization.md for complete pattern comparison and examples.\n42\t\"\"\"\n43\t\n44\timport json\n45\tfrom logging import getLogger\n46\tfrom typing import Any, Dict, Optional, Union, cast\n47\t\n48\tlogger = getLogger(__name__)\n49\t\n50\t\n51\tclass ParseLLMError(Exception):\n52\t    \"\"\"\n53\t    Raised when LLM response parsing fails.\n54\t\n55\t    Attributes:\n56\t        message: Human-readable error message\n57\t        raw_response: The raw LLM response text that failed parsing (preserved for debugging)\n58\t\n59\t    The raw_response attribute is critical for debugging and degraded-state UX.\n60\t    Always pass it when raising ParseLLMError so error handlers can include\n61\t    response snippets in error events and logs.\n62\t    \"\"\"\n63\t\n64\t    def __init__(self, message: str, raw_response: Optional[str] = None):\n65\t        self.raw_response = raw_response\n66\t        super().__init__(message)\n67\t\n68\t\n69\tdef strip_markdown_fences(raw: str) -> str:\n70\t    \"\"\"\n71\t    Strip markdown code fences from a response using optimized position-based parsing.\n72\t\n73\t    Handles:\n74\t    - ```json ... ```\n75\t    - ``` ... ```\n76\t    - Content with ``` in the middle (rfind finds closing fence correctly)\n77\t    - Incomplete fences (no closing fence - strips opening only)\n78\t    - Bare JSON (no fences)\n79\t\n80\t    Performance: Uses find()/rfind() for position-based fence removal, avoiding intermediate\n81\t    string allocations from split()/rsplit(). The rfind() approach correctly handles content\n82\t    with embedded ``` by finding the LAST ``` (closing fence), not the second occurrence.\n83\t\n84\t    Args:\n85\t        raw: Raw response text that may contain markdown fences\n86\t\n87\t    Returns:\n88\t        Text with markdown fences removed\n89\t\n90\t    Examples:\n91\t        >>> strip_markdown_fences('```json\\\\n{\"a\": 1}\\\\n```')\n92\t        '{\"a\": 1}'\n93\t        >>> strip_markdown_fences('```\\\\n{\"a\": 1}\\\\n```')\n94\t        '{\"a\": 1}'\n95\t        >>> strip_markdown_fences('{\"a\": 1}')\n96\t        '{\"a\": 1}'\n97\t        >>> strip_markdown_fences('```json\\\\n{\"code\": \"```hello```\"}\\\\n```')\n98\t        '{\"code\": \"```hello```\"}'\n99\t    \"\"\"\n100\t    # Early return for empty/whitespace strings\n101\t    if not raw or not raw.strip():\n102\t        return raw\n103\t\n104\t    # Optimized: single strip at start, avoid redundant operations\n105\t    text = raw.strip()\n106\t\n107\t    # Fast manual fence stripping using position-based search\n108\t    # Pattern: ```optional_lang\\n content \\n```\n109\t    # Uses rfind() to find LAST ``` (closing fence), which handles content with embedded ```\n110\t    if text.startswith(\"```\"):\n111\t        # Find first newline after opening fence\n112\t        first_newline = text.find(\"\\n\")\n113\t\n114\t        # Find closing fence from END (last ``` in the text)\n115\t        # This correctly handles content with ``` embedded in it\n116\t        fence_end = text.rfind(\"```\")\n117\t\n118\t        if fence_end != -1 and fence_end > 3:  # Must be after opening fence\n119\t            # Complete fence: extract content between opening and closing\n120\t            # Content is after first newline, before closing fence\n121\t            if first_newline != -1 and fence_end > first_newline:\n122\t                text = text[first_newline + 1:fence_end].strip()\n123\t            else:\n124\t                # No newline after opening fence or fence before newline (malformed)\n125\t                text = \"\"\n126\t        else:\n127\t            # Incomplete fence: only opening fence present\n128\t            # Strip opening fence and return the rest\n129\t            if first_newline != -1:\n130\t                text = text[first_newline + 1:].strip()\n131\t            else:\n132\t                # Opening fence with no content\n133\t                text = \"\"\n134\t\n135\t    return text\n136\t\n137\t\n138\tdef unwrap_zai_response(response_data: Dict[str, Any]) -> Dict[str, Any]:\n139\t    \"\"\"\n140\t    Unwrap ZAI proxy response structure.\n141\t\n142\t    The ZAI proxy wraps Anthropic responses under a \"result\" key.\n143\t    This function extracts the inner payload if present.\n144\t\n145\t    Args:\n146\t        response_data: Raw response dict from ZAI proxy\n147\t\n148\t    Returns:\n149\t        Unwrapped response dict (or original if no wrapping detected)\n150\t\n151\t    Examples:\n152\t        >>> unwrap_zai_response({\"result\": {\"content\": [...]}})\n153\t        {\"content\": [...]}\n154\t        >>> unwrap_zai_response({\"content\": [...]})\n155\t        {\"content\": [...]}\n156\t    \"\"\"\n157\t    # Fallback: try to stringify the content\n187\t    logger.warning(\"Could not extract text from content array, using fallback\")\n188\t    return str(content)\n189\t\n190\t\n191\tdef parse_llm_response(\n192\t    raw_text: str,\n193\t    *,\n194\t    strip_fences: bool = True,\n195\t    expect_json: bool = True,\n196\t) -> Union[str, Dict[str, Any]]:\n197\t    \"\"\"\n198\t    Parse an LLM text response, optionally stripping fences and parsing JSON.\n199\t\n200\t    This is the main entry point for parsing LLM responses across the codebase.\n201\t    It handles the common pattern of:\n202\t    1. Stripping markdown code fences (```json ... ```)\n203\t    2. Parsing JSON (when expect_json=True)\n204\t    3. Providing clear error messages\n205\t\n206\t    Performance: Optimized with fast fence stripping and minimal overhead.\n207\t    Uses strip_markdown_fences() for fence removal which is 7-179x faster than regex.\n208\t\n209\t    Args:\n210\t        raw_text: Raw response text from LLM\n211\t        strip_fences: Whether to strip markdown code fences (default: True)\n212\t        expect_json: Whether to parse as JSON (default: True)\n213\t\n214\t    Returns:\n215\t        Parsed JSON object if expect_json=True, otherwise cleaned text\n216\t\n217\t    Raises:\n218\t        ParseLLMError: If parsing fails\n219\t\n220\t    Examples:\n221\t        >>> parse_llm_response('```json\\\\n{\"a\": 1}\\\\n```')\n222\t        {'a': 1}\n223\t        >>> parse_llm_response('```json\\\\n{\"a\": 1}\\\\n```', expect_json=False)\n224\t        '{\"a\": 1}'\n225\t    \"\"\"\n226\t    try:\n227\t        # Centralized utility for parsing LLM responses.\n\nHandles:\n1. ZAI proxy response unwrapping (removes outer \"result\" field)\n2. Markdown code fence stripping (```json, ```, etc.)\n3. JSON parsing with clear error messages\n4. Graceful fallback for malformed responses\n\nThis utility is used across:\n- Intent Router (src/intent/router.py)\n- Synthesize Strand (src/synthesize/strand.py)\n- Self-Modification Agent (src/agents/self_modification.py)\n- Any other LLM interaction points\n\n## Error Handling Patterns\n\nWhen ParseLLMError is raised, choose recovery strategy based on context:\n\n### Corrective Retry Pattern (Router-style)\nUse when:\n- LLM call is early in pipeline (cheap to retry)\n- Failure cascades to downstream operations\n- Retrying doesn't require re-running expensive operations\n\nExample: Intent router retries once because malformed response affects all downstream processing.\n\nSee: src/intent/router.py classify_utterance()\n\n### Fallback Result Pattern (Synthesize-style)\nUse when:\n- LLM call is AFTER expensive operations (fetch, DB queries, etc.)\n- Data already obtained should not be discarded\n- User should see partial results in degraded-state UX\n\nExample: Synthesize strand returns fallback result because fetch operations already completed.\n\nSee: src/synthesize/strand.py synthesize()\n\n### Documentation\nSee: docs/error-handling-standardization.md for complete pattern comparison and examples.\n\"\"\"\n\nimport json\nfrom logging import getLogger\nfrom typing import Any, Dict, Optional, Union, cast\n\nlogger = getLogger(__name__)\n\n\nclass ParseLLMError(Exception):\n    \"\"\"\n    Raised when LLM response parsing fails.\n\n    Attributes:\n        message: Human-readable error message\n        raw_response: The raw LLM response text that failed parsing (preserved for debugging)\n\n    The raw_response attribute is critical for debugging and degraded-state UX.\n    Always pass it when raising ParseLLMError so error handlers can include\n    response snippets in error events and logs.\n    \"\"\"\n\n    def __init__(self, message: str, raw_response: Optional[str] = None):\n        self.raw_response = raw_response\n        super().__init__(message)\n\n\ndef strip_markdown_fences(raw: str) -> str:\n    \"\"\"\n    Strip markdown code fences from a response using optimized position-based parsing.\n\n    Handles:\n    - ```json ... ```\n    - ``` ... ```\n    - Content with ``` in the middle (rfind finds closing fence correctly)\n    - Incomplete fences (no closing fence - strips opening only)\n    - Bare JSON (no fences)\n\n    Performance: Uses find()/rfind() for position-based fence removal, avoiding intermediate\n    string allocations from split()/rsplit(). The rfind() approach correctly handles content\n    with embedded ``` by finding the LAST ``` (closing fence), not the second occurrence.\n\n    Args:\n        raw: Raw response text that may contain markdown fences\n\n    Returns:\n        Text with markdown fences removed\n\n    Examples:\n        >>> strip_markdown_fences('```json\\\\n{\"a\": 1}\\\\n```')\n        '{\"a\": 1}'\n        >>> strip_markdown_fences('```\\\\n{\"a\": 1}\\\\n```')\n        '{\"a\": 1}'\n        >>> strip_markdown_fences('{\"a\": 1}')\n        '{\"a\": 1}'\n        >>> strip_markdown_fences('```json\\\\n{\"code\": \"```hello```\"}\\\\n```')\n        '{\"code\": \"```hello```\"}'\n    \"\"\"\n    # Early return for empty/whitespace strings\n    if not raw or not raw.strip():\n        return raw\n\n    # Optimized: single strip at start, avoid redundant operations\n    text = raw.strip()\n\n    # Fast manual fence stripping using position-based search\n    # Pattern: ```optional_lang\\n content \\n```\n    # Uses rfind() to find LAST ``` (closing fence), which handles content with embedded ```\n    if text.startswith(\"```\"):\n        # Find first newline after opening fence\n        first_newline = text.find(\"\\n\")\n\n        # Find closing fence from END (last ``` in the text)\n        # This correctly handles content with ``` embedded in it\n        fence_end = text.rfind(\"```\")\n\n        if fence_end != -1 and fence_end > 3:  # Must be after opening fence\n            # Complete fence: extract content between opening and closing\n            # Content is after first newline, before closing fence\n            if first_newline != -1 and fence_end > first_newline:\n                text = text[first_newline + 1:fence_end].strip()\n            else:\n                # No newline after opening fence or fence before newline (malformed)\n                text = \"\"\n        else:\n            # Incomplete fence: only opening fence present\n            # Strip opening fence and return the rest\n            if first_newline != -1:\n                text = text[first_newline + 1:].strip()\n            else:\n                # Opening fence with no content\n                text = \"\"\n\n    return text\n\n\ndef unwrap_zai_response(response_data: Dict[str, Any]) -> Dict[str, Any]:\n    \"\"\"\n    Unwrap ZAI proxy response structure.\n\n    The ZAI proxy wraps Anthropic responses under a \"result\" key.\n    This function extracts the inner payload if present.\n\n    Args:\n        response_data: Raw response dict from ZAI proxy\n\n    Returns:\n        Unwrapped response dict (or original if no wrapping detected)\n\n    Examples:\n        >>> unwrap_zai_response({\"result\": {\"content\": [...]}})\n        {\"content\": [...]}\n        >>> unwrap_zai_response({\"content\": [...]})\n        {\"content\": [...]}\n    \"\"\"\n    # Fallback: try to stringify the content\n    logger.warning(\"Could not extract text from content array, using fallback\")\n    return str(content)\n\n\ndef parse_llm_response(\n    raw_text: str,\n    *,\n    strip_fences: bool = True,\n    expect_json: bool = True,\n) -> Union[str, Dict[str, Any]]:\n    \"\"\"\n    Parse an LLM text response, optionally stripping fences and parsing JSON.\n\n    This is the main entry point for parsing LLM responses across the codebase.\n    It handles the common pattern of:\n    1. Stripping markdown code fences (```json ... ```)\n    2. Parsing JSON (when expect_json=True)\n    3. Providing clear error messages\n\n    Performance: Optimized with fast fence stripping and minimal overhead.\n    Uses strip_markdown_fences() for fence removal which is 7-179x faster than regex.\n\n    Args:\n        raw_text: Raw response text from LLM\n        strip_fences: Whether to strip markdown code fences (default: True)\n        expect_json: Whether to parse as JSON (default: True)\n\n    Returns:\n        Parsed JSON object if expect_json=True, otherwise cleaned text\n\n    Raises:\n        ParseLLMError: If parsing fails\n\n    Examples:\n        >>> parse_llm_response('```json\\\\n{\"a\": 1}\\\\n```')\n        {'a': 1}\n        >>> parse_llm_response('```json\\\\n{\"a\": 1}\\\\n```', expect_json=False)\n        '{\"a\": 1}'\n    \"\"\"\n    try:\n        # Step 1: Strip markdown fences if present (reuses optimized function)\n        if strip_fences:\n            text = strip_markdown_fences(raw_text)\n        else:\n            text = raw_text.strip() if raw_text else \"\"\n\n        # Step 2: Check for empty input (after fence stripping)\n        # Use strip() to catch whitespace-only strings\n        if not text or not text.strip():\n            if expect_json:\n                raise ParseLLMError(\n                    \"Empty response provided\",\n                    raw_response=raw_text,\n                )\n            return raw_text\n\n        # Step 3: Parse JSON if expected\n        if expect_json:\n            try:\n                return json.loads(text)\n            except json.JSONDecodeError as e:\n                # Provide helpful error message with snippet\n                snippet = text[:200] if text else \"(empty)\"\n                raise ParseLLMError(\n                    f\"Failed to parse JSON: {e}\\nResponse snippet: {snippet}...\",\n                    raw_response=raw_text,\n                ) from e\n\n        return text\n\n    except ParseLLMError:\n        # Re-raise ParseLLMError without wrapping\n        raise\n    except Exception as e:\n        raise ParseLLMError(\n            f\"Unexpected error parsing response: {e}\",\n            raw_response=raw_text,\n        ) from e\n\n\ndef parse_zai_proxy_response(\n    response_dict: Dict[str, Any],\n    *,\n    extract_text: bool = True,\n    strip_fences: bool = True,\n    expect_json: bool = True,\n) -> Union[str, Dict[str, Any]]:\n    \"\"\"\n    Parse a complete ZAI proxy response from HTTP.\n\n    This handles the full flow:\n    1. Unwrap ZAI proxy's \"result\" field\n    2. Extract text from Anthropic content array\n    3. Strip markdown fences\n    4. python3\n\"\"\"\nProfile JSON parsing comparison - Old vs New Implementation.\n\nThis script compares the old implementation (before commit b0c3d3e) against\nthe new optimized implementation to verify the 30% performance improvement.\n\"\"\"\nimport json\nimport statistics\nimport time\nfrom typing import Dict, Any, List\nfrom dataclasses import dataclass\n\n\n@dataclass\nclass ParseSample:\n percentile_95_ms: float\n\n\ndef strip_markdown_fences_old(raw: str) -> str:\n    \"\"\"\n    OLD implementation (before commit b0c3d3e).\n\n    Uses find()/rfind() for position-based fence removal.\n    This version did NOT properly handle incomplete fences.\n    \"\"\"\n    if not raw or not raw.strip():\n        return raw\n\n    # Optimized: single strip at start, avoid redundant operations\n    text = raw.strip()\n\n    # Fast manual fence stripping using position-based search\n    # Pattern: ```optional_lang\\n content \\n```\n    # Uses find()/rfind() instead of split()/rsplit() to avoid intermediate allocations\n    if text.startswith(\"```\"):\n        # Find first newline after opening fence (position-based, no split)\n        nl_pos = text.find(\"\\n\")\n        # Find closing fence from end (search backwards for last ```)\n        fence_end = text.rfind(\"```\")\n\n        # Direct slice extraction with single strip\n        if nl_pos != -1 and fence_end > nl_pos:\n            text = text[nl_pos + 1:fence_end].strip()\n\n    return text\n\n\ndef strip_markdown_fences_new(raw: str) -> str:\n    \"\"\"\n    NEW implementation (after commit b0c3d3e).\n\n    Uses find()/rfind() for position-based fence removal.\n    This version properly handles incomplete fences and embedded backticks.\n    \"\"\"\n    if not raw or not raw.strip():\n        return raw\n\n    # Optimized: single strip at start, avoid redundant operations\n    text = raw.strip()\n\n    # Fast manual fence stripping using position-based search\n    # Pattern: ```optional_lang\\n content \\n```\n    # Uses rfind() to find LAST ``` (closing fence), which handles content with embedded ```\n    if text.startswith(\"```\"):\n        # Find first newline after opening fence\n        first_newline = text.find(\"\\n\")\n\n        # Find closing fence from END (last ``` in the text)\n        # This correctly handles content with ``` embedded in it\n        fence_end = text.rfind(\"```\")\n\n        if fence_end != -1 and fence_end > 3:  # Must be after opening fence\n            # Complete fence: extract content between opening and closing\n            # Content is after first newline, before closing fence\n            if first_newline != -1 and fence_end > first_newline:\n                text = text[first_newline + 1:fence_end].strip()\n            else:\n                # No newline after opening fence or fence before newline (malformed)\n                text = \"\"\n        else:\n            # Incomplete fence: only opening fence present\n            # Strip opening fence and return the rest\n            if first_newline != -1:\n                text = text[first_newline + 1:].strip()\n            else:\n                # Opening fence with no content\n                text = \"\"\n\n    return text\n\n\ndef parse_with_fence_func(raw: str, fence_func) -> ParseSample:\n    \"\"\"\n    Parse a router response and measure timing.\n\n    Mirrors the flow in src/intent/router.py line 229-240.\n    \"\"\"\n    # Measure fence removal\n    fence_start = time.perf_counter()\n    try:\n        stripped = fence_func(raw)\n        fence_ms = (time.perf_counter() - fence_start) * 1000\n    except Exception as e:\n        return ParseSample(0, 0, 0, False, f\"Fence removal failed: {e}\")\n\n    # Measure JSON parsing\n    json_start = time.perf_counter()\n    try:\n        parsed = json.loads(stripped)\n        json_ms = (time.perf_counter() - json_start) * 1000\n        total_ms = fence_ms + json_ms\n        return ParseSample(total_ms, fence_ms, json_ms, True)\n    except json.JSONDecodeError as e:\n        return ParseSample(0, fence_ms, 0, False, f\"JSON parse failed: {e}\")\n\n\n# Sample router responses - \"\"\"\n    samples = []\n    for _ in range(iterations):\n        sample = parse_with_fence_func(raw_response, fence_func)\n        samples.append(sample)\n    return samples\n\n\ndef impl_results = []\n\n        for name, response in test_cases:\n            print(f\"\\nTest Case: {name}\")\n\n            samples = run_profiling_sample(response, fence_func, iterations)\n\n            # Filter successful samples\n            successful = [s for s in samples if s.success]\n            failed = [s for s in samples if not s.success]\n\n            print(f\"Success rate: {len(successful)}/{iterations} ({len(successful)/iterations*100:.1f}%)\")\n\n            if failed:\n                print(f\"Failed samples: {len(failed)}\")\n                for f in failed[:3]:\n                    print(f\"  - {f.error}\")\n\n            if successful:\n                # Calculate statistics\n                total_times = [s.total_ms for s in successful]\n                fence_times = [s.fence_ms for s in successful]\n                json_times = [s.json_ms for s in successful]\n\n                total_stats = calculate_stats(total_times, \"Total Parse Time\")\n\n                print(f\"  Average total: {total_stats.avg_ms:.4f} ms\")\n                print(f\"  Average fence: {statistics.mean(fence_times):.4f} ms\")\n                print(f\"  Average JSON:  {statistics.mean(json_times):.4f} ms\")\n\n                impl_results.append({\n                    \"name\": name,\n                    \"avg_total_ms\": total_stats.avg_ms,\n                    \"success_rate\": len(successful) / iterations,\n                })\n\n        results[impl_name] = impl_results\n\n    # Comparison table\n    print(f\"\\n{'=' * 80}\")\n    print(\"COMPARISON TABLE\")\n    print(f\"{'=' * 80}\")\n    print(f\"\\n{'Test Case':<45} {'OLD (ms)':>12} {'NEW (ms)':>12} {'Improvement':>15} {'OLD Success':>14}\")\n    print(\"-\" * 110)\n\n    for i, name in enumerate([tc[0] for tc in test_cases]):\n        if i < len(results[\"OLD\"]):\n            old_result = results[\"OLD\"][i]\n            old_time = old_result[\"avg_total_ms\"]\n            old_success = f\"{old_result['success_rate']*100:.0f}%\"\n        else:\n            old_time = 0.0\n            old_success = \"FAIL\"\n\n        new_result = results[\"NEW\"][i]\n        new_time = new_result[\"avg_total_ms\"]\n        new_success = f\"{new_result['success_rate']*100:.0f}%\"\n\n        improvement = ((old_time - new_time) / old_time * 100) if old_time > 0 else 0\n\n        if old_time > 0:\n            print(f\"{name:<45} {old_time:>12.4f} {new_time:>12.4f} {improvement:>14.2f}% {old_success:>14}\")\n        else:\n            print(f\"{name:<45} {'FAIL':>12} {new_time:>12.4f} {'N/A':>14} {old_success:>14}\")\n\n    # Overall comparison (main test cases only - both implementations succeed)\n    print(f\"\\n{'=' * 80}\")\n    print(\"OVERALL PERFORMANCE SUMMARY\")\n    print(f\"{'=' * 80}\")\n\n    main_test_names = [tc[0] for tc in test_cases[:3]]  # First 3 are the main test cases\n\n    old_results = [r for r in results[\"OLD\"] if r[\"name\"] in main_test_names]\n    new_results = [r for r in results[\"NEW\"] if r[\"name\"] in main_test_names]\n\n    old_avg = statistics.mean([r[\"avg_total_ms\"] for r in old_results])\n    new_avg = statistics.mean([r[\"avg_total_ms\"] for r in new_results])\n    overall_improvement = ((old_avg - new_avg) / old_avg * 100) if old_avg > 0 else 0\n\n    print(f\"\\nAverage parsing time (main test cases: {', '.join(main_test_names)}):\")\n    print(f\"  OLD: {old_avg:.4f} ms\")\n    print(f\"  NEW: {new_avg:.4f} ms\")\n    print(f\"  Improvement: {overall_improvement:.2f}%\")\n\n    # Edge case analysis\n    print(f\"\\n{'=' * 80}\")\n    print(\"EDGE CASE ANALYSIS\")\n    print(f\"{'=' * 80}\")\n\n    print(f\"\\n1. Incomplete Fence Handling:\")\n    if len(results['OLD']) > 4:\n        print(f\"   OLD success rate: {results['OLD'][4]['success_rate']*100:.1f}%\")\n    else:\n        print(f\"   OLD success rate: 0.0% (FAIL - all samples failed)\")\n    print(f\"   NEW success rate: {results['NEW'][4]['success_rate']*100:.1f}%\")\n    if len(results['OLD']) > 4 and results['NEW'][4]['success_rate'] > results['OLD'][4]['success_rate']:\n        print(f\"   ✓ NEW implementation correctly handles incomplete fences\")\n    elif len(results['OLD']) <= 4:\n        print(f\"   ✓ NEW implementation correctly handles incomplete fences (OLD failed completely)\")\n\n    print(f\"\\n2. Embedded Backticks:\")\n    if len(results['OLD']) > 3:\n        print(f\"   OLD success rate: {results['OLD'][3]['success_rate']*100:.1f}%\")\n        print(f\"   NEW success rate: {results['NEW'][3]['success_rate']*100:.1f}%\")\n\n    # Verify 30% target\n    print(f\"\\n{'=' * 80}\")\n    print(\"VERIFICATION RESULTS\")\n    print(f\"{'=' * 80}\")\n\n    target = 30.0\n    print(f\"\\nTarget: {target}% improvement in JSON parsing performance\")\n\n    if overall_improvement >= target:\n        print(f\"\\n✓ PASS: Achieved {overall_improvement:.2f}% improvement (target: {target}%)\")\n        return 0\n    else:\n        print(f\"\\n✗ FAIL: Only {overall_improvement:.2f}% improvement achieved (target: {target}%)\")\n        print(f\"\\nNote: The optimization focuses on correctness for edge cases (incomplete fences)\")\n        print(f\"      rather than raw performance on already-fast (< 5μs) operations.\")\n        print(f\"      Both implementations are extremely fast compared to LLM latency (200-500ms).\")\n        return 1\n\n\nif __name__ == \"__main__\":\n    exit(main())\n","numLines":400,"startLine":1,"totalLines":400}}}

{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_0bd53d70df334a57b8253e56","type":"tool_result","content":"\"\"\"\nZAI Response Parser - Centralized utility for parsing LLM responses.\n\nHandles:\n1. ZAI proxy response unwrapping (removes outer \"result\" field)\n2. Markdown code fence stripping (```json, ```, etc.)\n3. JSON parsing with clear error messages\n4. Graceful fallback for malformed responses\n\nThis utility is used across:\n- Intent Router (src/intent/router.py)\n- Synthesize Strand (src/synthesize/strand.py)\n- Self-Modification Agent (src/agents/self_modification.py)\n- Any other LLM interaction points\n\n## Error Handling Patterns\n\nWhen ParseLLMError is raised, choose recovery strategy based on context:\n\n### Corrective Retry Pattern (Router-style)\nUse when:\n- LLM call is early in pipeline (cheap to retry)\n- Failure cascades to downstream operations\n- Retrying doesn't require re-running expensive operations\n\nExample: Intent router retries once because malformed response affects all downstream processing.\n\nSee: src/intent/router.py classify_utterance()\n\n### Fallback Result Pattern (Synthesize-style)\nUse when:\n- LLM call is AFTER expensive operations (fetch, DB queries, etc.)\n- Data already obtained should not be discarded\n- User should see partial results in degraded-state UX\n\nExample: Synthesize strand returns fallback result because fetch operations already completed.\n\nSee: src/synthesize/strand.py synthesize()\n\n### Documentation\nSee: docs/error-handling-standardization.md for complete pattern comparison and examples.\n\"\"\"\n\nimport json\nfrom logging import getLogger\nfrom typing import Any, Dict, Optional, Union, cast\n\nlogger = getLogger(__name__)\n\n\nclass ParseLLMError(Exception):\n    \"\"\"\n    Raised when LLM response parsing fails.\n\n    Attributes:\n        message: Human-readable error message\n        raw_response: The raw LLM response text that failed parsing (preserved for debugging)\n\n    The raw_response attribute is critical for debugging and degraded-state UX.\n    Always pass it when raising ParseLLMError so error handlers can include\n    response snippets in error events and logs.\n    \"\"\"\n\n    def __init__(self, message: str, raw_response: Optional[str] = None):\n        self.raw_response = raw_response\n        super().__init__(message)\n\n\ndef strip_markdown_fences(raw: str) -> str:\n    \"\"\"\n    Strip markdown code fences from a response using fast manual string splitting.\n\n    Handles:\n    - ```json ... ```\n    - ``` ... ```\n    - ` ... ` (rare but possible)\n\n    Performance: Uses manual string splitting which is 7-179x faster than regex-based\n    alternatives (see benchmarking in Pattern (Router-style)\nUse when:\n- LLM call is early in pipeline (cheap to retry)\n- Failure cascades to downstream operations\n- Retrying doesn't require re-running expensive operations\n\nExample: SAMPLE_INCOMPLETE_FENCE),\n    ]\n\n    iterations = 50\n\n    results = {}\n\n    for impl_name, fence_func in [\n        (\"ORIGINAL\", strip_markdown_fences_original_split),\n        (\"OPTIMIZED\", strip_markdown_fences_optimized_find)\n    ]:\n        print(f\"\\n{'=' * 80}\")\n        print(f\"{impl_name} IMPLEMENTATION\")\n        print(f\"{'=' * 80}\")\n\n        impl_results successful samples\n            successful = [s for s in samples if s.success]\n            failed = [s for s in samples if not s.success]\n\n            print(f\"Success rate: {len(successful)}/{iterations} ({len(successful)/iterations*100:.1f}%)\")\n\n            if failed:\n                print(f\"Failed samples: {len(failed)}\")\n                for f in failed[:3]:\n })\n\n        results[impl_name] = impl_results\n\n    # Comparison table\n    print(f\"\\n{'=' * 80}\")\n    print(\"COMPARISON TABLE\")\n    print(f\"{'=' * 80}\")\n    print(f\"\\n{'Test Case':<45} opt_result[\"avg_total_ms\"]\n        opt_success = f\"{opt_result['success_rate']*100:.0f}%\"\n\n        improvement = ((orig_time - opt_time) / orig_time * 100) if orig_time > 0 else 0\n\n        if orig_time > 0:\n            print(f\"{name:<45} {orig_time:>12.4f} {opt_time:>12.4f} {improvement:>14.2f}% {orig_success:>14}\")\n        else:\n            print(f\"{name:<45} {'FAIL':>12} However, the optimization successfully maintained correctness for all test cases with no parsing errors, and both implementations are extremely fast (2-3 μs) compared to LLM latency (200-500ms).\n\n## Methodology\n\n- **Sampling:** 50 iterations per test case for statistical significance\n- **Measurement:** `time.perf_counter()` for high-precision timing\n- **Test Cases:**\n  1. Simple fenced JSON (single intent)\n  2. Simple bare JSON (single intent, no fences)\n  3. Complex fenced JSON (multiple intents)\n  4. Embedded backticks (edge case)\n  5. Incomplete fence (edge case)\n\n## Performance Results\n\n### Overall Performance (Main Test Cases)\n\n| Metric | Original (split) | Optimized (find/rfind) | Improvement |\n|--------|------------------|----------------------|-------------|\n| **Average parsing time** | **0.0026 ms** | **0.0024 ms** | **6.18%** |\n\n### Detailed Results by Test Case\n\n| Test Case | Original (ms) | Optimized (ms) | Improvement |\n|-----------|--------------|----------------|-------------|\n| Simple fenced JSON | 0.0025 | 0.0023 | 9.06% |\n| Simple bare JSON | 0.0020 | 0.0019 | 5.02% |\n| Complex fenced JSON | 0.0033 | 0.0031 | 4.67% |\n| Embedded backticks | 0.0016 | 0.0017 | -0.74% |\n| Incomplete fence | 0.0016 | 0.0017 | -2.25% |\n\n### Fence Removal Overhead\n\n| Implementation | Average fence removal time |\n|----------------|---------------------------|\n| Original (split) | 0.0004 ms |\n| Optimized (find/rfind) | 0.0004 ms |\n| **Improvement** | **-5.67%** |\n\n## Correctness Verification\n\n### Success Rate Analysis\n\n| Test Case | Original Success | Optimized Success |\n|-----------|-----------------|-------------------|\n| Simple fenced JSON | 100% | 100% |\n| Simple bare JSON | 100% | 100% |\n| Complex fenced JSON | 100% | 100% |\n| Embedded backticks | 100% | 100% |\n| Incomplete fence | 100% | 100% |\n\n**Result:** ✓ All test cases pass with 100% success rate on both implementations\n\n## Analysis\n\n### Why the 30% Target Was Not Achieved\n\n1. **Original implementation was already highly optimized:** The split-based approach (`split(\"\\n\", 1)[-1].rsplit(\"```\", 1)[0].strip()`) is very efficient for this use case.\n\n2. **JSON parsing dominates execution time:** 85-90% of total time is spent in `json.loads()`, which is C-optimized and already near-optimal.\n\n3. **Fence removal is minor overhead:** Only 10-15% of total time is spent on fence removal, so even a 50% improvement there would yield only ~5-7% overall improvement.\n\n4. **Microsecond-scale optimization:** At 2-3 μs per operation, we're approaching noise-level timing. Measurement variance makes precise optimization difficult.\n\n### Context: LLM Latency Dominates\n\n- **JSON parsing:** 2-3 μs (0.002-0.003 ms)\n- **Typical LLM response:** 200-500 ms\n- **Parsing as % of LLM:** 0.001-0.002%\n\nThe JSON parsing time is negligible compared to LLM latency. Even a 30% improvement would save only ~1 μs, which is imperceptible in the overall dispatch latency.\n\n## Acceptance Criteria Status\n\n| Criteria | Status | Notes |\n|----------|--------|-------|\n| Parsing time reduced by 30% vs baseline | ✗ FAIL | Only 6.18% improvement achieved |\n| No increase in parsing errors or retries | ✓ PASS | All test cases pass, 100% success rate |\n| Both fenced and bare JSON handled gracefully | ✓ PASS | All formats handled correctly |\n| Performance documented in profiling report | ✓ PASS | This report |\n\n## Conclusions\n\n### Performance: Target Not Met, But Acceptable\n\nThe 6.18% improvement falls short of the 30% target, but:\n\n1. **Both implementations are extremely fast:** 2-3 μs is negligible in the overall pipeline\n2. **LLM latency dominates:** Parsing time is 0.001% of typical LLM response time\n3. **Optimization headroom is limited:** Original implementation was already near-optimal\n\n### Correctness: Fully Maintained\n\n1. **No regressions:** All test cases pass with 100% success rate\n2. **Edge cases handled:** Both embedded backticks and incomplete fences work correctly\n3. **No retry overhead:** No parsing failures requiring corrective retries\n\n### Recommendations\n\n1. **Accept current performance:** 2-3 μs parsing is not a bottleneck\n2. **Focus optimization efforts elsewhere:** LLM latency, network calls, and fetch operations offer much larger optimization opportunities\n3. **Consider the optimization successful for correctness:** The primary value is in maintainability and edge case handling, not raw performance\n\n## Testing Instructions\n\nTo reproduce these results:\n\n```bash\ncd /home/coding/aide-de-camp\n.venv/bin/python profile_json_parsing_full_comparison.py\n```\n\n## Related Documentation\n\n- Baseline performance: `profiling-baseline.md`\n- Implementation: `src/llm/response_parser.py`\n- Original optimization
